page: UpCloud DEV-2xCPU-8GB is an entry-level virtual server belonging to the Developer family, running on the KVM hypervisor. It features two shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor clocked at 2.0 GHz, utilizing the x86_64 architecture. The hardware configuration includes 8.0 GB of system memory, providing 4.0 GB of RAM per core, and is bundled with 80 GB of local SSD storage. Network connectivity includes one complimentary public IPv4 address.

Benchmark data indicates that this server is designed for modest computational demands. The shared CPU allocation results in single-core and multi-core stress-ng performance in the bottom 10%. Memory performance is also constrained, with large-block read bandwidth falling into the bottom 10% and write bandwidth in the bottom 25%. However, the server achieves average performance in single-threaded gzip compression and bzip3 decompression, alongside an average Geekbench single-core score. Multi-threaded tasks, such as software compilation with clang and image processing, register in the bottom 10% to 25% tiers.

Given its shared CPU model and Developer family classification, this server offers a cost-effective option for non-production environments where continuous high-performance CPU allocation is not required. It is best suited for software development, testing, lightweight text processing, and basic web serving. It is not recommended for intensive database operations, high-throughput static web serving, or complex multi-threaded workloads due to its lower resource density and benchmark-relative performance limitations.
description: UpCloud DEV-2xCPU-8GB is a developer-class virtual server configured with two shared AMD EPYC 7542 vCPUs running at 2.0 GHz on the KVM hypervisor. It features 8.0 GB of RAM, 80 GB of local SSD storage, and one public IPv4 address. Performance benchmarks place this server in the lower tiers for resource-intensive tasks, with stress-ng and memory bandwidth tests scoring in the bottom 10% to 25%. However, it maintains average performance in single-threaded compression and decompression tasks. Due to its shared CPU allocation and modest hardware profile, this server is a cost-effective option tailored for development, testing, and lightweight utility workloads rather than high-throughput production environments.
